title: Rain Dance
text:
The problem is that without fail, I will fall for each and every article that tells me rain is expected today.

I have been here 6 years and I’ve filled in more mm of precipitation from my tears.
Let’s actually call them out on this: An un-named gulf news source vs the NOAA Climate Data Online historic data summary from Jan. 1, 2020 until Aug. 24, 2025.


Code
import polars as pl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker

from datetime import datetime

DATA_SRC = "../dataset/4186532.csv"

"""
output of `head ../dataset/4186532.csv`
"STATION","NAME","DATE","PRCP"
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-01",
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-02",
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-03",
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-04",
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-05",
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-06",
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-07",
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-08",
"AEM00041217","ABU DHABI INTERNATIONAL, AE","2020-01-09",
"""
DATA_DTYPE = {
  "STATION": pl.Utf8,
  "NAME": pl.Utf8,
  "DATE": pl.Datetime,
  "PRCP": pl.Float32
}




Code
# Since I can't use a custom local mplstyle file, let's specify all our RC
# Params here:
import matplotlib as mpl

RC_PARAMS = {
    # Axes
    "axes.titlesize": "large",
    "axes.labelsize": "large",
    "axes.formatter.use_mathtext": True,

    # Lines / patches
    "lines.linewidth": 1.0,
    "lines.markersize": 10,
    "hatch.linewidth": 0.25,
    "patch.antialiased": True,

    # Ticks
    "xtick.top": False,
    "xtick.bottom": True,
    "xtick.major.size": 4.0,
    "xtick.minor.size": 2.0,
    "xtick.major.width": 0.5,
    "xtick.minor.width": 0.5,
    "xtick.direction": "out",
    "xtick.minor.visible": True,
    "xtick.major.top": False,
    "xtick.major.bottom": True,
    "xtick.minor.top": False,
    "xtick.minor.bottom": True,
    "xtick.major.pad": 5.0,
    "xtick.minor.pad": 5.0,
    "xtick.labelsize": "large",

    "ytick.labelsize": "large",
    "ytick.left": True,
    "ytick.right": False,
    "ytick.major.size": 4.0,
    "ytick.minor.size": 2.0,
    "ytick.major.width": 0.5,
    "ytick.minor.width": 0.5,
    "ytick.direction": "out",
    "ytick.minor.visible": True,
    "ytick.major.left": True,
    "ytick.major.right": False,
    "ytick.minor.left": True,
    "ytick.minor.right": False,
    "ytick.major.pad": 5.0,
    "ytick.minor.pad": 5.0,

    # Legend
    "legend.frameon": True,
    "legend.fontsize": 12,
    "legend.loc": "upper right",

    # Figure layout
    "figure.autolayout": False,
    "figure.constrained_layout.w_pad": 0.01,
    "figure.constrained_layout.h_pad": 0.01,

    # Fonts
    "mathtext.fontset": "cm",
    "font.family": "serif",
    "font.serif": ["cmr10"],
    "font.sans-serif": ["cmr10"],

    # Saving figures
    "path.simplify": True,
}

mpl.rcParams.update(RC_PARAMS)


Let’s read this into a dataframe and plot all the times it’s rained in the last 6 years.


Code
df = pl.read_csv(
  DATA_SRC, separator=',', has_header=True, schema_overrides=DATA_DTYPE
)
# Fill in the empty prcp columns with zeros
df = df.with_columns(
  pl.col("PRCP").fill_null(0)
)


::: {#cell-Precipitation Over Jan. 1, 2020 - Aug. 24, 2025 .cell execution_count=4}

Code
fig = plt.figure(dpi=180)
plt.plot(df["DATE"], df["PRCP"])
plt.title("Precip. over 6-ish years")
rain_day_2024 = df.filter(
  pl.col("DATE") == datetime(2024, 4, 16)
).head(1)
plt.axvspan(xmin=datetime(2024,1,1), xmax=datetime(2024, 5, 1), color="#6400ff", alpha=0.3, label="Most rain ever,\n Spring 2024")
plt.annotate(
  text="That day it rained so hard\neverything blew away in the wind.\nApr. 16, 2024",
  xy=(rain_day_2024["DATE"][0], rain_day_2024["PRCP"][0]),
  xytext=(datetime(2022, 1, 1), 40),
  arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->")
)
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Precipitation (mm)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)





Precipitation Over Jan. 1, 2020 - Aug. 24, 2025



:::




Code
rained_days = df.filter(
  pl.col("PRCP") > 0.0
)

pl.Config.set_tbl_rows(30)
pl.Config.set_tbl_cols(20)
pl.Config.set_tbl_width_chars(140)
pl.Config.set_tbl_formatting("ASCII_MARKDOWN")
pl.Config.set_fmt_str_lengths(80)
pl.Config.set_float_precision(3)

# print(rained_days.select(["NAME", "DATE", "PRCP"]))
print(f"\n\nOver the period of time between "
      f"{df['DATE'].head(1)[0]} and "
      f"{df['DATE'].tail(1)[0]}..."
      f"({len(df['DATE']):,} days),"
      "\nIt has rained a total of "
      f"{rained_days.shape[0]} times."
)




Over the period of time between 2020-01-01 00:00:00 and 2025-08-24 00:00:00...(2,043 days),
It has rained a total of 22 times.





According to news websites whose names I won’t mention ahem ahem rhymes with shmulf bews ahem ahem, I should expect rain almost once a week! I should be expecting it in the middle of Abu Dhabi July and packing a jacket just in case! I should be cancelling outdoor dates and carrying my umbrella everywhere I go… yeah sure.
SO, let’s learn how to (hopefully ethically) scrape their news articles for mentions of rain over the current decade and come up with a percentage likelihood for how much rain we’re expected to see according to each article that mentions rain.
The sitemap for gulfnews.com looks like this: https://gulfnews.com/sitemap/sitemap-daily-2026-02-21.xml
Each day, an entry. I just need to extract the contents of each day’s headlines and filter for keywords like ‘rain’, ‘rainfall’, ‘precipitation’, etc. and then save articles that match into a jsonl file sorted by month.
After python-ing with gpt5 on opencode using xmls and beautifulsoup4, I get something that looks like this:






Note



{“url”: “https://gulfnews.com/uae/weather/uae-weather-update-rain-thunder-and-lightning-expected-to-hit-the-country-starting-monday-1.1709394185115”, “lastmod”: “2024-03-03”, “title”: “UAE weather update: Rain, thunder and
lightning expected to hit the country starting Monday”, “title_matches_keywords”: true, “text”: “““Abu Dhabi: The National Center of Meteorology (NCM) has stated that the UAE is being affected by a surface low-pressure system extending from the Southwest, accompanied by a cold air mass from the West in the upper atmosphere, resulting in a flow of clouds..On Monday and Tuesday, cloud cover is expected to gradually increase from the afternoon into the evening from the West, extending across scattered areas of the country. This will be accompanied by convective clouds, associated with rainfall of varying intensities, and at times heavy with lightning and thunder in some areas..Click here to get exclusive content with Gulf News WhatsApp channel.By Wednesday, the amount of clouds and rainfall is forecast to decrease. However, the weather is expected to remain partly cloudy to cloudy at times, particularly in southern regions, with a decrease in temperatures..The sea conditions are expected to be moderate to rough, especially with the presence of convective clouds in the Arabian Gulf and the Oman Sea.”““}


For each url found in the day’s sitemap, we skim the title to see if anything relates to weather. If it does, go to the url and extract the article body to form this jsonl file. The format looks like this:
The main function in charge of doing this is def extract_sitemap_urls_to_jsonl() in ../main.py


Code
import sys
from pathlib import Path

sys.path.append(str(Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

from main import *
def extract_sitemap_urls_to_jsonl(
    start_date,
    end_date,
    output_root=OUTPUT_ROOT,
    request_delay_s=0.5,
    force=False,
):
    output_path = None
    header = (
        f"{ANSI_INFO}╔══════════════════════════════════════════════╗{ANSI_RESET}\n"
        f"{ANSI_INFO}║{ANSI_RESET} {ANSI_BOLD}Gulf News Extraction Console{ANSI_RESET}           {ANSI_INFO}║{ANSI_RESET}\n"
        f"{ANSI_INFO}╚══════════════════════════════════════════════╝{ANSI_RESET}"
    )
    print(header, flush=True)

    # We'll generate a sitemap url based on the format of the website:
    #    "https://gulfnews.com/sitemap/sitemap-daily-2026-02-21.xml"
    prefix = "https://gulfnews.com/sitemap/sitemap-daily"

    current_date = parse_date(start_date)
    end = parse_date(end_date)

    while current_date < end:
        sitemap_url = f"{prefix}-{current_date.isoformat()}.xml"
        sitemap_date = current_date.isoformat()
        output_path = output_path_for_sitemap(sitemap_date, output_root=output_root)
        # This prevents us from re-bothering the server for data we've already
        # collected, unless the force option is passed.
        if output_path.exists() and not force:
            return output_path

        xml_text = fetch_text(sitemap_url)
        entries = parse_sitemap(xml_text)
        total = len(entries)
        counter = 0
        print(
            f"{ANSI_INFO}[INFO]{ANSI_RESET} "
            f"Date: {ANSI_BOLD}{sitemap_date}{ANSI_RESET} | "
            f"Entries: {ANSI_BOLD}{total}{ANSI_RESET}",
            flush=True,
        )

        # Make a different JSONL for each month of entries that've been filtered
        # through.
        output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

        matches = 0
        with output_path.open("w", encoding="utf-8") as handle:
            for entry in entries:
                counter += 1
                progress = f"{counter}/{total}" if total else "0/0"
                print(
                    f"\r{ANSI_FETCH}[FETCH]{ANSI_RESET} "
                    f"{ANSI_BOLD}{progress}{ANSI_RESET} "
                    f"{ANSI_DIM}{current_date}{ANSI_RESET}",
                    end="",
                    flush=True,
                )
                html_text = fetch_text(entry.url)
                soup = BeautifulSoup(html_text, "html.parser")
                title = extract_title(soup)
                if title == "":
                    print(f"\n{ANSI_WARN}[WARN]{ANSI_RESET} Empty title", flush=True)
                matched = title_matches_keywords(title)
                if matched:
                    matches += 1
                    article_text = extract_article_text(soup)
                else:
                    article_text = None
                handle.write(
                    json.dumps(
                        {
                            "url": entry.url,
                            "lastmod": (
                                entry.lastmod.isoformat() if entry.lastmod else None
                            ),
                            "title": title,
                            "title_matches_keywords": matched,
                            "text": article_text,
                        }
                    )
                    + "\n"
                )
                if request_delay_s:
                    sleep(request_delay_s)
            print(
                f"\r{ANSI_DONE}[DONE]{ANSI_RESET} Day complete with #{matches=}",
                flush=True,
            )

        current_date += timedelta(days=1)

    return output_path


What we end up with is a directory tree full of jsonl files
data
└── gulfnews
    └── articles
        ├── 2020-01
        │   ├── sitemap-2020-01-01.jsonl
This process is very slow since we’re fetching each url from each day one at a time, we can parallelize this process by using threads or asyncio but we shouldn’t do that wantonly. Hitting a news site with dozens of requests per second is not very polite and respectful. So instead, we’re going to parallelize ethically by limiting our overall HTTP calls.
We want to now do some analysis on the article text and title.

        f"{ANSI_INFO}╔══════════════════════════════════════════════╗{ANSI_RESET}\n"
        f"{ANSI_INFO}║{ANSI_RESET} {ANSI_BOLD}Gulf News Extraction Console{ANSI_RESET}           {ANSI_INFO}║{ANSI_RESET}\n"

        # through.

            for entry in entries:
                counter += 1

                    f"\r{ANSI_FETCH}[FETCH]{ANSI_RESET} "

                    end="",
